mnemonics.title: DN Structure
mnemonics.content: DN = RDN + RDN... (from leaf to root): Think 'Leaves to Root' - Like addressing: House → Street → City → Country

mnemonics.title: LDAP Operations
mnemonics.content: CRUD for LDAP: Add, Delete, Modify, Rename, Bind, Search, Compare - Remember as 'Add Del Mod Ren Bin Sea Com'

mnemonics.title: Common LDAP Attributes
mnemonics.content: CN=Common Name, OU=Organizational Unit, DC=Domain Component, O=Organization, C=Country - 'Cows Eat Outside'

acronyms.usage: Protocol for accessing directory services over TCP/IP networks
Unique identifier for directory entries following the hierarchy from root to leaf
Single attribute that uniquely identifies an entry among siblings
Hierarchical tree structure organizing directory entries
Individual entry in the directory containing attributes
Permissions defining who can access directory objects
Server in Kerberos that issues authentication tickets
Authentication allowing access to multiple applications with one credential
Attribute used to represent domain names in directory paths

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted tree with root at top, branches as OUs (Organizational Units), and leaves as individual entries (users, computers). Like an organizational chart.
Think of DN as a file path: /root/ou=HR/ou=Users/cn=John (but read bottom-to-top, right-to-left in LDAP notation).
Draw flowchart: Client → Bind (Auth) → Search/Modify → Unbind. Like a login → do work → logout cycle.

visualTips.visualize: Draw multiple servers with bidirectional arrows showing data flow between them, like a mesh network.

analogies.analogy: Like a phone book for an organization - you look up a name (user) to find their details (email, phone, department).
Like the protocol for calling directory assistance - you ask (search) and get information back.
Like a file system hierarchy - /company/department/user.txt, but inverted and for network resources.
Like showing your ID at a security checkpoint - you prove your identity before accessing resources.
Like a database schema - defines what fields/columns and what data types are allowed for each record type.
Like having multiple copies of an important document in different offices - same info, multiple locations for access.
